---
title: "Capstone — Open Bayesian Modelling with Uncertainty"
subtitle: "Choose a model · fit it · quantify uncertainty · report results + ethics"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

The capstone is the open finale: there is **no answer key**. On a new dataset you choose a
model, fit it by any sound method, **quantify the uncertainty** with a Bayesian approach from
Unit 4 or Unit 5, and write a short report on what you found, how confident you are, and the
**ethics of acting on it**. You are graded not on hitting a number but on *method validity*,
*internal consistency*, *runnable code*, and the *quality of your reasoning*.

## Learning objectives

By the end of the Capstone you will be able to:

- **Fit a model** that captures the signal in the data. *(Apply)*
- **Quantify uncertainty** with a valid Bayesian method. *(Analyze)*
- **Produce runnable, reproducible code** to the contract. *(Apply)*
- **Interpret your results (CERL)** and assess the **ethics/impact** of acting on them.
  *(Evaluate/Create)*

## What you'll do

The dataset `data/capstone.csv` has inputs $x\in[-3,3]$ with a deliberate **gap in
$[0.5, 2.0]$** and noisy outputs $t$. Implement the `BayesianModel` contract in `hw.py`
(`fit`, `predict`, `predictive_std`, `posterior_cov`) **with the model and Bayesian method of
your choice**, then write the report. Pick any uncertainty method you were taught — this is a
menu, not a fence:

- **Bayesian linear regression** — conjugate, closed-form posterior + predictive variance
  (**Unit 4**).
- **Laplace approximation** — a Gaussian fit at the MAP (**Unit 5**).
- **MCMC posterior predictive** — propagate parameter draws through the model (**Unit 5**).

Any valid method that **propagates parameter uncertainty** is acceptable. **Seed** any RNG, and
don't hard-code outputs — the autograder checks *properties of your method*, not a specific
value.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** The exact/conjugate methods of Unit 4 (predictive variance, Beta–Binomial)
and the approximate methods of Unit 5 (Laplace, MCMC); how to propagate parameter uncertainty
into a predictive band.

**Connections.**

> This synthesizes the Bayesian half of the course — any of the **Unit 4 / Unit 5** uncertainty
> methods is fair game. In grading terms the Capstone **gates the A** (you need it on top of
> all six core units) and elsewhere counts as one core unit of lift at the B/C boundaries.

## How it's graded

Specifications grading, **adapted for an open task**: because there is no reference answer, the
**Correctness** (core/gateway) bundle assesses **method validity, internal consistency, and
runnable code** — every spec passes for any sound Bayesian approach and fails a degenerate one.
**Interpretation** is CERL on your results **and a required ethics note**; **Process** covers
seeding and a clean render. Any unmet bundle gets one **revise-and-resubmit**. The Capstone also
**gates the A** and can lift a borderline grade — full policy in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — the `BayesianModel` contract (`fit`, `predict`,
  `predictive_std`, `posterior_cov`) and `load_data` (signatures unchanged; any RNG seeded).
- Your **report (~1 page)** — *I1 results* (the fit; where and why uncertainty is large or
  small; your method's behaviour in the $[0.5, 2.0]$ gap and why it does or doesn't grow there;
  the real limits) and *I2 ethics/impact* (who is affected by the uncertainty, what acting on
  over-confident regions risks, and one safeguard).
- Your **rendered notebook** with the ±2σ predictive-band figure.

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **8–12 hours [PROPOSED]** — open-ended: choosing and fitting a model, getting a valid
uncertainty band, and writing a careful results-plus-ethics report.
